definition: Commodity-inflation transmission operates through tiered lags — energy (1-3 months), food (3-12 months), services/wages (6-36 months) — but specific timing estimates carry wide confidence intervals (90% CI for energy-to-CPI spans 1-14 months) that are functionally uninformative for most decision horizons.
evidence: 2021-23 sequence: goods inflation peaked Q1 2022, services ex-shelter peaked Q3 2022 (~6-month lag), shelter peaked Q1 2023 (~12-month lag)
Firm-level three-tier decomposition (0-1, 2-4, 4-8 quarters) validates top-down CPI lag structure from bottom up
General ordering confirmed across 7 historical episodes; specific timing varies by episode
90% CI for energy-to-CPI passthrough timing spans 1-14 months per challenger_02 analysis
open_questions: Can higher-frequency data narrow timing confidence intervals?
Does the ordering remain stable across different commodity types (energy vs metals vs agriculture)?
How do supply chain changes (nearshoring, JIT→JIC) affect lag structure?
